title: The Millstone and the Little Ones
subtitle: Christ warns against scandalising the small in faith

stillness: Be still, and consider one small soul.
Whatever befalls the just man shall not grieve him.
Let your heart grow quiet before the Lord who watches over the little ones.
gospel: Whoever shall scandalise one of these little ones who believe in me,
it were better for him that a millstone be hung about his neck,
and that he be drowned in the depth of the sea.
Woe to the world because of scandals.
For it must needs be that scandals come;
but nevertheless, woe to that man by whom the scandal cometh.
behold: Consider who these little ones are:
not little in age, but in faith — newly converted, newborn in Christ.
They are called little because, humbling themselves down from proud heights,
by patience and humility they willingly become small and lowly.
They are still weak in the faith,
and so can easily be drawn, by evil example, to things forbidden.
The lesser ones are quickly scandalised;
the greater do not so easily take offence.
Behold the millstone — the stone the ass or horse turns with the wheel in the mill;
by it is meant any heavy and weighty stone.
See how he who poisons a fountain is the cause of death
to all who drink of it and die;
so it is also with scandal.
consider: He who scandalises gives another, by word or deed not rightly done, occasion of ruin into sin;
and by word or by example drives him toward evil.
As those who honour these little ones for God's sake shall have their reward,
so those who dishonour them — that is, scandalise them — shall bear the last vengeance.
Far better is it to end this temporal life by the heaviest punishment
than, by scandalising and destroying a brother, to merit eternal death;
more to be chosen is it to be afflicted here in time than to be tormented for ever.
This too may be taken of the burden of sin:
the millstone is the weight of penance, the sea is the bitterness of contrition.
Better for the sinner to be drowned with heavy penance in the deep of bitter and heartfelt compunction
than to be reserved for eternal damnation.
Whoever weighs this carefully will never find any burden of penance heavy — but rather very light.
The scandal of prelates and of the religious is more perilous and more grave,
for their deed is the more drawn into example.
And though one may sometimes do scandal usefully,
he will not be judged by God according to the good that comes of it,
but according to the end the scandaliser intended.
Good was the crucifixion of Christ; yet they who crucified him did not do well.
God ever judges the work from the wickedness of the sinner, not from the good that follows.
Learn from the punishment of the scandaliser the prize of him who saves:
were the salvation of one soul not exceedingly dear to him, he would not threaten so great a punishment.
If a man or a woman adorn themselves and draw the eyes of beholders,
though no harm come of it, they shall yet suffer judgment for ever;
for they have offered poison, even if there was none to drink it —
since beauty pours poison into souls.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, good Master and ruler,
govern and rule mercifully all that is within me and without,
that never may scandal come from others to me, or from me to others;
but by your grace cut away and shut out from me every occasion of scandal.
Grant me also that I may presume to despise none of the little or the small ones,
but rather strive to honour them all —
those whom you so deigned to honour
that you appointed the blessed Angels to their service and keeping,
and you yourself, to save them, willed to come from heaven into the world and to die.

latinJewel.latin: Sicut enim, qui intoxicaret fontem, causa esset mortis omnium potantium et morientium de eo; sic est et de scandalo.
latinJewel.english: For just as one who would poison a fountain would be the cause of death to all who drink of it and die thereof, so it is also with scandal.
